channel.title: Comments for page "Uncle Jeff"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Uncle Jeff"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you somehow made the embodiment of evil…likable. somehow. now i sort of want jeff to just take a nap

item.title: Necropost
item.content:encoded:
What part of "terms you're comfortable with" did you not get?

The gist of his advice is to solve your problems with conflict and violence, regardless of how justifiable it might be to the reader given the protagonists situation.
He could very well be telling her to eviscerate her boyfriend a.nd leave his corpse upon the pavement as a warning to her neighbors, but who would be comfortable following that advice?

He indicates that he is not going to destroy the world but is instead going to "watch smart young people like you learn how to become the gods they've always dreamed of being". But hes not really watching her now is he?
He's guiding her.
To violence.
And what is concerning is his claim of being the creepy uncle of the whole Universe. Who is to say he isn't giving the same advice to her boyfriend?

More importantly, what is a creepy uncle? Is it the uncle that tries to look up your skirt at a family reunion like in Madea’s Family Reunion? His advice/requests for her seem like sound advice on how to make real positive changes, which is perhaps nobler then asking for someone to bend over the grab them a soda to keep the simile going, but hes not a good uncle, hes a creepy one who has some sort of alternate motive. Much like in Family Reunion, and given his admission of being interested in watching the world not devouring it, I'd wager he's looking to watch her, and the universe at large, get violent for his amusement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I spent half of this article thinking this was a Jeff the Killer crossover for some reason… just by the name and the first few sentences.

Even though I started piecing together who this was about once 7 chains were mentioned, I still don't think it works. It's a cute little thought to entertain (this giant world-eater being some girl's self-help counselor), but I don't think it works. 2317 (if I got the # right) works because nobody knows what will happen when the seventh chain breaks, although it's strongly implied that it's going to be bad. This story just sucks the gravity out of the situation, even though it's not supposed to be taken that seriously.

I think this might've worked with a lot of dangerous SCPs, but 2317 isn't one of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I guess thousands of years in an extradimensional prison can change your outlook on things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw, hell, I liked this enough to give it an upvote. The Scarlet King as a young girl/goddess' motivational speaker, not to mention the way you pulled it off, solidified this +1. Don't spend it all on candy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, it's certainly unique. I can see while people might not like it, and I think there's a few grammar mistakes, but it was funny and enjoyable enough for me to upvote.

But you have any plans to travel to Eastern Europe in the next week that I haven't noticed

^
Shouldn't there be an "unless" there?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That doesn't change the fact that 2317 is being someone's kindly uncle. The problem still applies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. The Jeff, Devourer of Worlds? I guess I can dig it, though I'm curious as to what your thought process was here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He'll really cut his way inside your head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude. Duuuuuuuuuude. Jason Voorhees as a guidance counselor would be the literal best.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see what you're getting at, so I should probably clarify: the girl's receiving this in terms she can understand through English vernacular.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get that Jeff is supposed to be 2317 - but an unknowable cosmic horror offering life advice to a teenage girl can only offer pathos when we have very little prior knowledge of said cosmic horror. This is like turning Jason Voorhees into a guidance counselor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See, that's not helpful. Magic teenage girls are, like, 1/3 of the characters on this site.

I just don't see him having any taste for human music, sorry. Erich Zann, maybe, but not many others.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He's talking to a teenage girl. But thanks for telling me - I added a line clarifying it.

And he loves GWAR you heathen

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Who is Jeff talking to? I figured out who Jeff is (side note: I don't think he listens to Gwar, a point I think we disagree on), but I'm not sure I've got a handle on who this is directed at.
